dating.display: composed c. 2nd century BCE – 2nd century CE (within Mahābhārata book 6); traditional setting: the Kurukṣetra war
dating.basis: Language, doctrinal synthesis (Sāṅkhya, Yoga, bhakti), and position within the epic's textual history; the critical edition (BORI) frames the text's layered growth.

positions.position: c. 2nd c. BCE – 2nd c. CE composition
positions.evidence: Language and doctrine relative to datable texts.

positions.position: Traditional: spoken by Kṛṣṇa at Kurukṣetra (traditional epoch 3102 BCE)
positions.evidence: The Kali-yuga epoch; reported as tradition, not as a composition date.

summary: Seven hundred verses on a battlefield: Arjuna's collapse before killing kin, and Kṛṣṇa's reply — a synthesis of duty (karma-yoga), knowledge (jñāna), and devotion (bhakti) that became Hinduism's most read, most translated, most commented text, from Śaṅkara's commentary to Gandhi's.
whyItMatters: The Gītā is the tradition's portable core — the text through which most of the modern world meets Hindu thought (first English translation: Wilkins, 1785, the first Sanskrit work printed in English). Its reception history alone spans Thoreau, Oppenheimer's misquoted 'destroyer of worlds', and India's independence movement.
honestyNote: Oppenheimer's famous line is a real (post-hoc, self-reported) allusion but circulates with fabricated embellishments; and the '5,000-year-old Gītā' framing states the traditional epoch as a composition date — see the dating positions.

sources.label: van Buitenen, J.A.B., The Bhagavadgītā in the Mahābhārata (Chicago, 1981)
Malinar, A., The Bhagavadgītā: Doctrines and Contexts (Cambridge, 2007)
Wilkins, C. (tr.), The Bhăgvăt-Gēētā (1785) — the reception landmark
Davis, R., The Bhagavad Gita: A Biography (Princeton, 2014) — reception history
Sukthankar, V.S. et al., Mahābhārata critical edition (BORI, 1933–66)
Gita Supersite (IIT Kanpur) — critical text, 20+ commentaries, multiple translations

dharmicTie.statement: Smṛti with near-śruti standing: one of the three prasthānas (with Upaniṣads and Brahmasūtras) on which every Vedānta school must comment.

scriptureRefs.text: karmaṇy evādhikāras te — the charter verse of karma-yoga

dating.display: layered composition; extant redaction c. 1st–3rd century CE, with material plausibly reaching back toward the Mauryan period (4th c. BCE)
dating.basis: Statistical and content analysis of the text's layers (Trautmann); Olivelle's redactional history; the traditional attribution ties it to Candragupta Maurya's minister.

positions.position: Composite text, final form early centuries CE
positions.evidence: Layer analysis; vocabulary; institutional references postdating the Mauryas.

positions.position: Substantially the work of Kauṭilya/Cāṇakya, 4th c. BCE
positions.holders: Traditional attribution; some earlier Indology (Kangle defends a unified early text)
positions.evidence: Internal attribution verses; Kangle's rebuttals to layer analysis.

summary: A cold-eyed manual of statecraft rediscovered in 1905 (Shamasastry's Mysore manuscript): taxation, espionage, diplomacy (the maṇḍala theory of neighboring states), law, urban administration, even workplace regulations — realpolitik with checklists, often compared to Machiavelli and usually harder-headed.
whyItMatters: It falsifies the cliché that classical India produced only otherworldly speculation: here is empirical, amoral administrative science. Its rediscovery reshaped the study of ancient Indian polity, and its dating debate is a model case of tradition vs text-criticism, held openly.

sources.label: Olivelle, P., King, Governance, and Law in Ancient India: Kauṭilya's Arthaśāstra (OUP, 2013)
Trautmann, T., Kauṭilya and the Arthaśāstra (Brill, 1971)
Kangle, R.P., The Kauṭilīya Arthaśāstra, 3 vols. (Bombay, 1960–65)

dharmicTie.statement: Statecraft inside the dharmic knowledge frame: the text's own curriculum for the king places the trayī (the three Vedas) among the four sciences, and its law sections work the same terrain as dharmaśāstra.

scriptureRefs.text: ānvīkṣikī, trayī, vārttā, daṇḍanīti — the four vidyās, the Veda among them

dating.display: layered: Agniveśa core (older), Caraka's redaction c. 1st–2nd century CE, Dṛḍhabala's completion c. 4th–5th century CE
dating.basis: The text names its own redactional history; Meulenbeld's survey synthesizes the dating.

summary: The medical counterpart to Suśruta's surgery: internal medicine (kāyacikitsā) in eight books — etiology, diagnosis, pharmacology (hundreds of plant drugs), dietetics, ethics of practice (including a physician's oath), and an explicitly empirical temper: 'the science of life is verified by direct observation, inference, and authority, and yukti — reasoned judgment.'
whyItMatters: Foundational text of Āyurveda's rational-medicine stream, and evidence of institutionalized medical education, ethics, and pharmacological method in classical India. Its honest framing matters: a historical medical system to be studied and mined for leads — not a certified modern pharmacopoeia.
honestyNote: Classical Āyurveda's humoral framework (tridoṣa) is a pre-modern theory; individual drugs and practices need modern trials on their own merits (some pass, many untested). Both 'ancient quackery' and 'complete science of everything' framings fail the record — see ledger category 9 and 12 boundaries.

sources.label: Meulenbeld, G.J., A History of Indian Medical Literature, Vol. IA (1999)
Sharma, P.V. (ed./tr.), Caraka Saṃhitā, 4 vols. (Chaukhambha, 1981–94)
Wujastyk, D., The Roots of Ayurveda (Penguin, 2003)

image.alt: Sanskrit palm-leaf medical manuscript (13th-c. Cikitsāsārasaṅgraha of Vaṅgasena) — illustrative of the āyurvedic manuscript tradition

dharmicTie.statement: The rational-medicine stream of Āyurveda, framed by the text itself as knowledge descending through a ṛṣi lineage (Brahmā → Prajāpati → Aśvins → Indra → Bharadvāja) — the tradition's standard way of situating a science within dharma.

dating.display: c. 325–425 CE (Maas); traditional attributions much earlier
dating.basis: Philipp Maas's text-historical work dates the sūtras with their Bhāṣya as a unit; older scholarship spread dates from 2nd c. BCE to 4th c. CE.

positions.position: c. 325–425 CE, sūtras and Bhāṣya composed together (Pātañjalayogaśāstra)
positions.evidence: Manuscript colophons, citation history, doctrinal cross-references.

positions.position: Sūtras centuries earlier than the commentary; or Patañjali = the grammarian (2nd c. BCE)
positions.evidence: The grammarian identification is traditional; text-critics find it unsupported.

summary: 195 aphorisms systematizing yoga as an eight-limbed discipline (yama through samādhi) on a Sāṅkhya metaphysical base — the classical philosophy of meditative practice, with almost nothing about physical postures: āsana gets three sūtras, defined simply as a steady, comfortable seat.
whyItMatters: The intellectual charter of yoga as a darśana, and the text every modern yoga lineage cites. The gap between its three āsana sūtras and the modern postural universe is itself historically important — documented in the modern-yoga entry (Early Modern era) and the through-line below.

chain: Pātañjala yoga (4th c. CE): meditative eight-limb system
Haṭha corpus (c. 11th–15th c. CE: Amṛtasiddhi, Haṭhapradīpikā) develops postures, breath, and body techniques
19th–20th c. revival hybridizes haṭha with modern physical culture (Krishnamacharya's Mysore lineage and others)
Global postural yoga, which cites Patañjali as charter while practicing a substantially modern synthesis — both halves documented

sources.label: Mallinson, J. & Singleton, M., Roots of Yoga (Penguin, 2017)
Singleton, M., Yoga Body: The Origins of Modern Posture Practice (OUP, 2010)
Maas, P., "A Concise Historiography of Classical Yoga Philosophy", in Periodization and Historiography of Indian Philosophy (2013)
Bryant, E., The Yoga Sūtras of Patañjali (North Point, 2009)
Mallinson, J. & Singleton, M., Roots of Yoga (2017)
Singleton, M., Yoga Body (2010)
White, D.G., The Yoga Sutra of Patanjali: A Biography (Princeton, 2014)

dharmicTie.statement: One of the six darśanas of Hindu philosophy; the yoga it systematizes is already defined in the Upaniṣads.

scriptureRefs.text: the earliest formal definition of yoga as the steady holding of the senses

dating.display: 499 CE (self-dated: composed when the author was 23, in Kali year 3600)
dating.basis: The text dates itself (Kālakriyā 10); one of the firmest dates in early Indian science.

summary: 121 verses that compress a mathematical-astronomical system: place-value arithmetic with an alphabetic numeral code, π as 3.1416 explicitly called 'approximate' (āsanna), a sine-difference table, the kuṭṭaka algorithm for linear indeterminate equations — and the Earth rotating on its axis, with the sky's westward wheel explained as relative motion, like a boat rider seeing the bank move.
whyItMatters: The fountainhead of the classical Indian astronomy schools, transmitted into Arabic (as 'Arjabhar') in the 8th century — a genuine link in the chain that carried Indian mathematical astronomy west. His rotation thesis, argued and then rejected by his own successors, is a case study of internal scientific debate.
honestyNote: Āryabhaṭa taught rotation, not heliocentrism — planets still orbit a central Earth in his system. 'Āryabhaṭa discovered heliocentrism before Copernicus' is a ledger-grade overclaim; the sourced claim (axial rotation, relative motion, deliberate approximation of π) is remarkable enough.

locations.name: Kusumapura/Pāṭaliputra (Patna, Bihar, India) — his stated location

sources.label: Shukla, K.S. & Sarma, K.V., Āryabhaṭīya of Āryabhaṭa: Critical Edition and Translation (INSA, 1976)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009), ch. 4
Pingree, D., "The Greek Influence on Early Islamic Mathematical Astronomy" and jyotiṣa survey articles — Arabic transmission
Indian Journal of History of Science (INSA) — Āryabhaṭa studies; INSA published the 1976 critical edition

dharmicTie.statement: Jyotiṣa in the vedāṅga lineage, carried by a self-consciously dharmic scholarly culture — the treatise opens with an invocation to Brahman before turning to mathematics.

scriptureRefs.text: praṇipatyaikam anekam — bowing to the One who is also many, the true deity

dating.display: c. 375–415 CE (reign of Candragupta II, by the inscription's palaeography and content)
dating.basis: The pillar's six-line Sanskrit inscription eulogizes a king 'Candra'; palaeography and content point to Candragupta II Vikramāditya. Originally erected as a Viṣṇu standard, later moved to the Qutb complex.

summary: A 7.2 m, ~6-tonne forge-welded wrought-iron column that has stood in the open for 1,600 years with only superficial rust. The mechanism is understood: high phosphorus (no manganese/sulfur control), Delhi's dry climate, and a self-healing protective layer of iron hydrogen phosphate ('misawite') characterized by IIT Kanpur metallurgists.
whyItMatters: A masterpiece of Gupta-era ferrous metallurgy — the largest hand-forged iron object of antiquity — and the atlas's model for handling 'mystery' framing: the pillar is extraordinary AND fully explained by materials science. Wonder and explanation are not opponents.
honestyNote: 'Rust-proof miracle metal beyond modern science' is a recurring overclaim; the corrosion resistance is published, peer-reviewed metallurgy (and the buried base does corrode). The achievement is the smiths', not the supernatural's.

sources.label: Balasubramaniam, R., "On the corrosion resistance of the Delhi iron pillar", Corrosion Science 42 (2000)
Balasubramaniam, R., Delhi Iron Pillar: New Insights (IIAS, 2002)
Fleet, J.F., Corpus Inscriptionum Indicarum III (1888) — the inscription
Archaeological Survey of India — monument records, Qutb complex

dharmicTie.statement: The object's own inscription is the tie: the pillar was erected as a dhvaja (standard) of Bhagavān Viṣṇu on Viṣṇupada hill by a king whose devotion it records in Sanskrit verse. This is not an inference — it is carved into the artifact.

scriptureRefs.ref: The pillar's Sanskrit inscription (c. 4th–5th c. CE)
scriptureRefs.text: erected as viṣṇor dhvaja on Viṣṇupada hill — a Vaiṣṇava votive monument

custody.place: Qutb complex, Mehrauli, Delhi — moved there in the medieval period from its original site (Viṣṇupada hill, location debated; Udayagiri is the leading candidate)
custody.legalBasis: Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains (AMASR) Act, 1958; within the Qutb Minar World Heritage property (1993). Its presence in a mosque complex is itself medieval history: relocated as spolia — documented, not euphemized.

parallels.name: Heliodorus pillar (Garuḍa-dhvaja)
parallels.note: a Greek ambassador's Vaiṣṇava votive column, 2nd c. BCE
